| Topic | Scope |
|---|---|
| Topic 1: Platforms & Integrations _(Stratum: 12.76%)_ | Cross-platform support, SDKs, connectors, cloud services, and external system integrations. |
| Topic 2: Embeddings & Retrieval _(Stratum: 8.76%)_ | Vector databases, embedding generation, indexing, similarity search, and RAG retrieval pipelines. |
| Topic 3: Model–Agent–Tool Interaction Contracts (Prompting & Parsing) _(Stratum: 6.00%)_ | Failures to follow structured interaction contracts, including prompt formats, tool calling schemas,<br>action inputs, multimodal inputs, and output parsing. |
| Topic 4: Version-Update Regressions _(Stratum: 3.87%)_ | Bugs and behavior changes introduced by library, framework, or model version upgrades. |
| Topic 5: Dependencies, Installs, Imports, Environments _(Stratum: 5.88%)_ | Package installation issues, dependency conflicts, import errors, environment setup, and version pinning. |
| Topic 6: Workflow, UI & API Behavior _(Stratum: 14.24%)_ | Workflow logic, user interface behavior, REST/SDK API usage, request/response handling, and UX issues. |
| Topic 7: Feature Requests & Configurability _(Stratum: 6.75%)_ | Requests for new features, enhancements, configuration options, and extensibility. |
| Topic 8: Tracebacks & Unhandled Exceptions _(Stratum: 4.10%)_ | Crashes, stack traces, uncaught exceptions, and errors directly surfaced to users or logs. |
| Topic 9: LangChain Runtime & Integration Breakages _(Stratum: 5.86%)_ | Runtime failures, breaking changes, and integration issues specific to the LangChain ecosystem. |
| Topic 10: Policy & Template Enforcement Friction _(Stratum: 8.91%)_ | Prompt policies, guardrails, templates, schema enforcement, validation constraints, and compliance friction. |
| Topic 11: Runtime Execution & Operational Failures (Docker, CLI, Headless) _(Stratum: 6.32%)_ | Execution failures in deployment and operations, including Docker, CLI tools, servers, and headless runs. |
| Topic 12: Agent Orchestration & Invocation Semantics _(Stratum: 7.60%)_ | Multi-agent coordination, sequencing, routing, invocation order, concurrency, and orchestration logic. |
| Topic 13: Provider / Model Inconsistencies & Tooling Limits _(Stratum: 8.97%)_ | Provider- or model-specific behavior differences, capability gaps, API mismatches, and tooling limitations. |

### 3.1 RQ1: Taxonomy of Challenges

To address RQ1, complementary topic taxonomies were constructed from Stack Overflow questions and GitHub issues related to AI Agent development. Both platforms capture developer-reported problems: Stack Overflow questions describe difficulties, while GitHub issues document failures, limitations, or unexpected behavior. The extracted topics are therefore interpreted as recurring technical challenges encountered by Agent developers. At the first level, fine-grained, topic taxonomies were derived. Stack Overflow yielded seven topics comprising 28 subtopics (ST; Table [2](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#S3.T2 "Table 2 ‣ 3 Results ‣ What Challenges Do Developers Face in AI Agent Systems? An Empirical Study on Stack Overflow & GitHub Issues")), reflecting common difficulties related to usage and understanding. GitHub yielded thirteen issue topics (GT; Table [3](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#S3.T3 "Table 3 ‣ 3 Results ‣ What Challenges Do Developers Face in AI Agent Systems? An Empirical Study on Stack Overflow & GitHub Issues")) that capture recurring implementation, integration, and maintenance problems reported across Agent frameworks. For clearer interpretation and systematic comparison across platforms, these fine-grained topics were further organized into five higher-level families of major Agent challenges: dependency and environment management, retrieval and memory pipelines, orchestration and execution control, interaction contracts, and runtime reliability. These families form a hierarchical abstraction that groups related Stack Overflow (ST) and GitHub (GT) topics by their shared underlying technical difficulty rather than their platform-specific surface manifestations. The five challenge families were derived through open coding and consensus-based synthesis over the extracted topics. Two authors independently examined topic titles, top keywords, and a stratified sample of 15 representative Stack Overflow posts or GitHub issues per topic to identify the core difficulty each topic represents. Based on this material, the authors independently grouped ST and GT topics into higher-level challenge families, focusing on root technical concerns rather than platform-specific phrasing. Inter-rater agreement was assessed using Cohen’s κ\\kappa = 0.825, indicating substantial agreement. Remaining disagreements were resolved through discussion with a third author to ensure conceptual consistency. Across both data sources, the most prominent challenge families consistently involve environment and dependency management (ST5; GT1, GT4, GT5), retrieval and knowledge integration within RAG pipelines (ST2, ST6; GT2, GT13), and Agent orchestration and execution control (ST4; GT12). Interaction contracts, which cover prompting, tool invocation, and structured outputs, form another major family (ST7; GT3, GT10), while runtime reliability and operational robustness issues frequently arise during execution and deployment (ST1, ST3; GT8, GT9, GT11). Collectively, these families provide a unified, hierarchical view of the technical challenges that characterize contemporary AI Agent development.

Major Challenge 1: Environment, Platforms, and Dependency Management.
This challenge concerns configuring execution environments, resolving dependency conflicts, and integrating Agent frameworks with external platforms and services. It is Agent-specific because Agents combine multiple tightly coupled components—models, tools, memory stores, runtimes, and UI or service layers—where version drift or platform mismatches can break end-to-end execution. On Stack Overflow, this challenge appears primarily as _Installation & Dependency Conflicts_ (ST5), where developers report failures caused by framework API churn or incompatible library upgrades (e.g., [Q77338572](https://stackoverflow.com/q/77338572 ""), [Q78613825](https://stackoverflow.com/q/78613825 "")). In contrast, GitHub issues emphasize platform-level and integration failures encountered during real deployments, including _Platforms & Integrations_ (GT1), _Dependencies, Installs, Imports, Environments_ (GT5), and _Version-Update Regressions_ (GT4). Representative examples include cross-platform runtime and SDK integration failures in OpenHands (e.g., [I11521](https://github.com/OpenHands/OpenHands/issues/11521 "")), environment and import errors preventing Agent initialization in AutoGen (e.g., [I2756](https://github.com/microsoft/autogen/issues/2756 "")), and breaking changes introduced by LangChain upgrades that invalidate existing Agent pipelines (e.g., [I23517](https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchain/issues/23517 "")). This contrast highlights how Stack Overflow reflects setup-time confusion, while GitHub exposes deployment-time fragility and integration risk in production Agent systems.

Major Challenge 2: Retrieval, Embeddings, and Agent Memory.
AI Agents rely on retrieval pipelines and persistent memory to ground decisions across multiple steps and interactions, making retrieval quality and state management central concerns. This challenge goes beyond general LLM applications, which typically retrieve context once per request, whereas Agents repeatedly query, update, and reason over stored knowledge. On Stack Overflow, these difficulties surface primarily as _Document Embeddings & Vector Stores_ and _RAG Engineering_ (ST2, ST6), where developers struggle with document chunking strategies, indexing semantics, and performance bottlenecks in vector stores (e.g., [Q78015622](https://stackoverflow.com/q/78015622 ""), [Q78505822](https://stackoverflow.com/q/78505822 "")). These discussions reflect design-time uncertainty about how to structure and persist Agent memory. In contrast, GitHub issues emphasize deployment-time and framework-level failures captured by _Embeddings & Retrieval_ (GT2) and _Provider / Model Inconsistencies & Tooling Limits_ (GT13). Representative issues include embedding and retrieval mismatches in LlamaIndex that break Agent memory pipelines (e.g., [I13488](https://github.com/run-llama/llama_index/issues/13488 "")) and provider-specific behavior that causes inconsistent retrieval results across Agent runs (e.g., [I5980](https://github.com/langflow-ai/langflow/issues/5980 "")). Together, these findings show that retrieval in Agent systems is not merely a modeling concern but a core operational dependency that directly affects planning correctness, tool invocation, and long-horizon Agent behavior.

Major Challenge 3: Orchestration and Execution Control.
This challenge captures how Agents coordinate tools, regulate execution flow, and maintain control across multi-step or multi-Agent workflows. Orchestration is inherently Agent-specific because it governs delegation, sequencing, and execution boundaries beyond a single LLM invocation. On Stack Overflow, this challenge appears as _Orchestration & Workflow Control_ (ST4), where developers raise questions about controlling tool invocation policies and diagnosing missing or ambiguous observability in complex multi-Agent workflows (e.g., [Q79332599](https://stackoverflow.com/q/79332599 ""), [Q79771469](https://stackoverflow.com/q/79771469 "")). In contrast, GitHub issues reveal orchestration breakdowns that emerge during real-world execution, captured by _Agent Orchestration & Invocation Semantics_ (GT12). For example, Semantic Kernel reports failures where Agents surface function-call intents during streaming but do not execute them, leading to stalled execution despite correct tool configuration (e.g., [I9245](https://github.com/microsoft/semantic-kernel/issues/9245 "")). This contrast highlights how Stack Overflow surfaces design-time uncertainty and observability gaps, while GitHub exposes execution-time orchestration failures in deployed Agent systems.

Major Challenge 4: Interaction Contracts Between Models and Tools.
AI Agents depend on strict interaction contracts governing prompts, tool calls, parameter schemas, and structured outputs. These contracts are critical because even minor violations can halt planning loops, break execution, or cause silent failures that are difficult to diagnose. On Stack Overflow, such issues are discussed under _Prompt & Output Engineering_ (ST7), where developers report difficulties enforcing JSON schemas, constraining model outputs, and reliably triggering tool calls (e.g., [Q77227902](https://stackoverflow.com/q/77227902 ""), [Q77362103](https://stackoverflow.com/q/77362103 "")). These discussions emphasize prompt design and output validation as mechanisms for maintaining contract compliance at development time. GitHub issues reveal the downstream consequences of contract violations in deployed systems, captured by _Model–Agent–Tool Interaction Contracts_ (GT3). For example, in RagFlow, a framework upgrade removed an expected tool-side parameter class, causing agents to fail at runtime due to broken assumptions about tool interfaces (e.g., [I9141](https://github.com/infiniflow/ragflow/issues/9141 "")). Similarly, LlamaIndex users report the inability to constrain generated entity and relation types when constructing knowledge graphs, highlighting the absence of enforceable schemas between model outputs and downstream tool expectations (e.g., [I10120](https://github.com/run-llama/llama_index/issues/10120 "")). Together, these issues show how interaction contracts evolve from a prompt-level usability concern on Stack Overflow into a correctness, robustness, and versioning challenge in real-world Agent systems.

Major Challenge 5: Runtime Reliability and Operational Robustness.
The final challenge concerns keeping AI Agents reliable during execution, deployment, and long-running operation. Agents are particularly vulnerable because they execute over extended periods, invoke external services, and maintain evolving state. On Stack Overflow, this challenge appears in _Operational Issues (Runtime & Integration)_ and _Robustness, Reliability & Evaluation_ (ST1, ST3), for example when permission or configuration errors invalidate evaluations (e.g., [Q76815315](https://stackoverflow.com/q/76815315 "")) or malformed tool outputs repeatedly break execution (e.g., [Q79134666](https://stackoverflow.com/q/79134666 "")). GitHub issues emphasize the operational consequences of these problems, captured by _Tracebacks & Unhandled Exceptions_ (GT8), _Runtime Execution & Operational Failures_ (GT11), and ecosystem-specific breakages such as LangChain runtime failures (GT9). Representative examples include repeated crashes in MetaGPT deployments (e.g., [I1862](https://github.com/FoundationAgents/MetaGPT/issues/1862 "")), execution failures in OpenHands during long-running tasks (e.g., [I907](https://github.com/OpenHands/OpenHands/issues/907 "")), and unhandled runtime errors in LangChain Agent pipelines (e.g., [I7900](https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchain/issues/7900 "")).

Workflow, UI & API Behavior (GT6) constitutes the largest stratum in the GitHub Issues dataset (14.24%), underscoring its importance in practice. These issues primarily fall under Challenge 3 and Challenge 1, as they concern how Agent workflows are composed, triggered, and executed through user interfaces and APIs. Most cases arise from UI-centric Agent platforms—particularly langgenius/dify—where Agents are built and deployed via visual workflows rather than code alone (e.g., [I15785](https://github.com/langgenius/dify/issues/15785 "")). This reflects a shift toward production-grade, user-facing Agent systems, where orchestration complexity and integration failures increasingly surface at the workflow and interface layer.

Feature Requests & Configurability (GT7) covers a unique but related set of Agent challenges, making up 6.75% of GitHub issues. Rather than focusing on bugs or failures, these issues show that developers often need more ways to extend, customize, or control how Agents work and fit into their infrastructure. Many of these requests come up after Agents are used in real-world settings, where the built-in features no longer meet all needs. For example, developers might want more configuration options, better API access, or easier ways to connect Agents with existing company systems. One case is developers using langgenius/dify who asked for extra OpenAPI endpoints so they could manage Agent annotations through code, pointing to a gap between what the UI can do and what automation requires (see [I6878](https://github.com/langgenius/dify/issues/6878 "")). Most of these issues relate to Challenge 1 (integration with environments and platforms) and Challenge 3 (control over orchestration and execution), since they are about making Agent systems more adaptable and manageable beyond default setups. The rise of GT7 shows that as Agent platforms get more advanced, developers spend less time fixing basic errors and more time seeking flexibility, compatibility, and maintainability for the long term.

Findings (AI Agent Topics).
AI Agent development involves a distinct and recurring set of technical challenges that cannot be fully explained by general LLM-based application development. These challenges span environment setup and dependency management, retrieval and memory pipelines, multi-agent orchestration, model–tool interaction contracts, runtime reliability, and workflow- and configuration-level concerns. Their recurrence indicates that Agent-specific difficulty arises not only from autonomy, tool-mediated execution, and long-running behavior, but also from the need to compose, customize, and govern Agents through user interfaces, APIs, and extensible configurations rather than isolated framework flaws or misuse.

### 3.2 RQ2: Popularity & Difficulty

To address RQ2, we analyze the relative popularity and difficulty of AI Agent challenges using complementary signals from Stack Overflow and GitHub issues.

Popularity of Agent Challenges (Stack Overflow).
Developer attention on Stack Overflow is concentrated around a small set of recurring topics (Table [4](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#S3.T4 "Table 4 ‣ 3.2 RQ2: Popularity & Difficulty ‣ 3 Results ‣ What Challenges Do Developers Face in AI Agent Systems? An Empirical Study on Stack Overflow & GitHub Issues")). The most popular challenges are _Installation & Dependency Conflicts_ (ST5), followed by _Prompt & Output Engineering_ (ST7) and _Document Embeddings & Vector Stores_ (ST2). These topics reflect common entry points into Agent development, where practitioners seek help configuring environments, controlling Agent behavior, and enabling retrieval-based memory. In contrast, _Orchestration_ (ST4) and _RAG Engineering_ (ST6) receive comparatively less attention, suggesting that advanced Agent workflows affect a smaller, more specialized subset of developers.

Difficulty of Agent Challenges (Stack Overflow).
Difficulty on Stack Overflow follows a different pattern. The hardest topics are _RAG Engineering_ (ST6), _Document Embeddings & Vector Stores_ (ST2), and _Orchestration_ (ST4), which exhibit long resolution times and a high share of unanswered questions. These challenges involve multi-step reasoning, persistent state, and coordination across tools, capabilities that are intrinsic to Agent systems. By contrast, although _Installation & Dependency Conflicts_ (ST5) is the most frequently discussed topic, it is among the easiest to resolve, indicating that such issues are widespread but relatively well understood once the root cause is identified.

Issue Volume and Resolution Difficulty (GitHub).
GitHub issues capture a different perspective, reflecting engineering workload and maintenance burden in production Agent frameworks. The largest issue volumes are observed for _Workflow, UI & API Behavior_ (GT6), _Platforms & Integrations_ (GT1), and _Policy & Template Enforcement Friction_ (GT10), highlighting the operational challenges of deploying user-facing, platform-integrated Agents. Resolution difficulty is most pronounced for _Policy & Template Enforcement Friction_ (GT10), _Agent Orchestration & Invocation Semantics_ (GT12), and _Platforms & Integrations_ (GT1), which exhibit high open-issue rates and prolonged activity. In contrast, topics such as _Workflow, UI & API Behavior_ (GT6) and _Feature Requests & Configurability_ (GT7) tend to close quickly, indicating that while frequent, these issues are often localized and easier for maintainers to address.

Stack Overflow vs. GitHub Comparison.
Taken together, the two platforms reveal complementary views of Agent difficulty. Stack Overflow highlights where developers struggle conceptually and during early development, with retrieval, orchestration, and RAG emerging as the hardest topics despite limited visibility. GitHub, in contrast, exposes where Agent systems fail at scale: orchestration semantics, policy enforcement, and platform integration translate into sustained maintenance effort and unresolved issues. Notably, _Orchestration_ shifts from being niche-but-hard on Stack Overflow (ST4) to a mid-to-high volume and difficult topic on GitHub (GT12), reflecting its growing importance as Agents move from prototypes to production systems.

https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2/FPopularity-FDifficulty.pngFigure 1: Stack Overflow Fused Popularity & Fused Difficulty.
https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2/issues_open_vs_total_by_topic.pngFigure 2: GitHub Issues Topics.

Findings (Popularity & Difficulty).
Easy-to-fix configuration and prompting issues dominate attention, while retrieval- and orchestration-centric challenges are less visible but substantially harder. As Agent systems transition into production, these harder challenges reappear on GitHub as prolonged operational and maintenance burdens, underscoring that Agent difficulty stems from autonomy, statefulness, and tool-mediated execution rather than isolated implementation errors.

Popularity–Difficulty Relationship.
Correlation analysis (Table [6](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#S3.T6 "Table 6 ‣ 3.2 RQ2: Popularity & Difficulty ‣ 3 Results ‣ What Challenges Do Developers Face in AI Agent Systems? An Empirical Study on Stack Overflow & GitHub Issues")) reveals a moderate negative relationship between popularity and difficulty: topics that attract more discussion generally resolve faster. Notably, comment activity is significantly associated with shorter time-to-accept, suggesting that collective discussion accelerates convergence on solutions. However, several Agent-specific challenges—most notably _RAG Engineering_ and _Orchestration_—remain difficult despite sustained attention, underscoring their intrinsic complexity.

### 3.3 RQ3: Domains of the AI Agent Ecosystem

To address RQ3, we organized the GitHub repositories from Table [8](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#S3.T8 "Table 8 ‣ Domain-Level Difficulty and Resolution Dynamics. ‣ 3.3 RQ3: Domains of the AI Agent Ecosystem ‣ 3 Results ‣ What Challenges Do Developers Face in AI Agent Systems? An Empirical Study on Stack Overflow & GitHub Issues") into seven ecosystem domains according to their functional roles and intended uses within the AI Agent landscape. These domains illustrate the positioning of Agent technologies in the ecosystem, spanning core orchestration frameworks, memory components, visual workflow platforms, and end-user Agent applications. We assigned domains through a collaborative, consensus-driven qualitative analysis of the repositories listed in Table [8](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#S3.T8 "Table 8 ‣ Domain-Level Difficulty and Resolution Dynamics. ‣ 3.3 RQ3: Domains of the AI Agent Ecosystem ‣ 3 Results ‣ What Challenges Do Developers Face in AI Agent Systems? An Empirical Study on Stack Overflow & GitHub Issues"). Three authors jointly examined each repository’s README file, description, and, when available, official project documentation or website to determine its primary purpose, target users, and architectural role. Additionally, we reviewed a random sample of 15 GitHub issues per repository to identify typical usage scenarios and failure modes. Drawing on this material, we iteratively discussed and refined domain boundaries until reaching agreement for all repositories. We selected this consensus-based approach because ecosystem domains are interpretive constructs that depend on repository intent and usage context rather than isolated technical features. The resulting seven domains represent a structured synthesis grounded in repository documentation, observed issue patterns, and shared expert judgment.

UI / Copilot Components focus on embedding Agents into interactive user experiences. Although represented by a single primary repository (CopilotKit, Table [8](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#S3.T8 "Table 8 ‣ Domain-Level Difficulty and Resolution Dynamics. ‣ 3.3 RQ3: Domains of the AI Agent Ecosystem ‣ 3 Results ‣ What Challenges Do Developers Face in AI Agent Systems? An Empirical Study on Stack Overflow & GitHub Issues")), this domain shows a high concentration of issues related to _Workflow, UI & API Behavior_ (GT6) and _Runtime Execution & Operational Failures_ (GT11). This indicates that even thin UI layers amplify reliability and integration problems once Agents operate in real-time, user-facing settings.

Memory & State Components, represented primarily by mem0, are dominated by _Embeddings & Retrieval_ (GT2) and _Provider / Model Inconsistencies_ (GT13). These challenges stem from maintaining persistent Agent memory across providers and execution contexts, and they tend to persist longer due to the architectural nature of state management.

Multi-Agent Coordination Frameworks, including AutoGen, CrewAI, and MetaGPT, exhibit heavy concentration in _Agent Orchestration & Invocation Semantics_ (GT12) and _Dependencies, Installs, Imports, Environments_ (GT5), with several topics exceeding one thousand issues. Delegation, shared memory, and inter-Agent communication amplify failure modes that are difficult to localize and resolve.

Core Orchestration Frameworks (e.g., langchain, langgraph, semantic-kernel) form the backbone of the ecosystem and account for some of the largest issue volumes overall. Their dominant challenges include _Platforms & Integrations_ (GT1), _Version-Update Regressions_ (GT4), and _Agent Orchestration & Invocation Semantics_ (GT12). These issues exhibit long activity spans, highlighting the fragility introduced by API churn in foundational Agent infrastructure.

RAG & Knowledge Systems, led by ragflow and llama\_index, are heavily dominated by _Embeddings & Retrieval_ (GT2) and _Policy & Template Enforcement Friction_ (GT10), each with several thousand issues. This domain underscores that retrieval quality, grounding guarantees, and policy enforcement remain persistent technical barriers for knowledge-intensive Agents.

Visual Workflow Platforms constitute the largest domain by issue volume, driven primarily by langgenius/dify, langflow, and Flowise. Challenges are overwhelmingly concentrated in _Workflow, UI & API Behavior_ (GT6) and _Feature Requests & Configurability_ (GT7). These issues tend to resolve quickly, suggesting that low-code Agent builders generate frequent but localized problems as users assemble production workflows through graphical abstractions.

Agent Apps (End-User) aggregate failures across the entire stack. Repositories such as lobe-chat and OpenHands each report well over one thousand issues, spanning _Platforms & Integrations_ (GT1), _Runtime Execution & Operational Failures_ (GT11), _Provider / Model Inconsistencies_ (GT13), and _Tracebacks & Unhandled Exceptions_ (GT8). This domain exposes how upstream design decisions manifest as user-visible failures when Agents are deployed as full applications.

#### Domain-Level Difficulty and Resolution Dynamics.

Table [10](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#S3.T10 "Table 10 ‣ Domain-Level Difficulty and Resolution Dynamics. ‣ 3.3 RQ3: Domains of the AI Agent Ecosystem ‣ 3 Results ‣ What Challenges Do Developers Face in AI Agent Systems? An Empirical Study on Stack Overflow & GitHub Issues") highlights systematic differences in how challenges evolve across Agent ecosystem domains. _UI / Copilot Components_ exhibit the highest open-issue rate, reflecting the difficulty of stabilizing interactive Agent behavior amid rapid UI iteration. _Core Orchestration Frameworks_ show the longest activity spans and time-to-close, consistent with the architectural depth of orchestration logic and the need for careful regression control. In contrast, _Visual Workflow Platforms_ and _RAG & Knowledge Systems_ tend to close issues quickly, indicating that many reports are incremental or configuration-driven. _Agent Apps (End-User)_ combine moderate open rates with long activity spans, suggesting that application-level Agents surface compounded failures originating across multiple upstream domains. Overall, Agent difficulty varies systematically by domain role rather than uniformly across the ecosystem.

Findings (Domains).
The AI Agent ecosystem is structurally heterogeneous: infrastructure-oriented domains concentrate fewer but harder orchestration and integration challenges, while UI- and workflow-oriented domains generate large volumes of faster-resolving issues. End-user Agent applications amplify challenges across all domains, confirming that Agent-specific difficulty arises from autonomy, persistent state, and cross-system coordination rather than isolated framework defects.

## 4 Discussion

#### Evolution of AI Agent Challenges.

Across both Stack Overflow and GitHub, the evolution of AI Agent challenges reflects a clear shift from early adoption hurdles to system-level complexity. In early 2023, Stack Overflow activity rises sharply around _Installation & Dependency Conflicts_ (ST5), _Document Embeddings & Vector Stores_ (ST2), and _RAG Engineering_ (ST6), indicating that developers initially struggled to assemble and operationalize emerging Agent stacks. This phase coincides with strong GitHub signals around _Runtime Execution & Operational Failures_ (GT11) and _Provider / Model Inconsistencies_ (GT13), suggesting that early Agent frameworks were fragile and highly sensitive to environment configuration and provider behavior. From late 2023 into early 2024, several foundational challenges stabilize. Dependency-related Stack Overflow questions flatten, and GitHub issues related to runtime failures (GT11) peak and then decline, reflecting maturation of core orchestration frameworks and more reliable deployment practices. In contrast, _Platforms & Integrations_ (GT1) remain persistently high throughout the entire period, highlighting that integrating Agents with external services, SDKs, and cloud environments remains a durable source of difficulty even as frameworks mature internally.
Beginning in mid-2024, the dominant challenges shift toward control, policy, and workflow semantics. GitHub issues related to _Policy & Template Enforcement Friction_ (GT10) rise steadily, reflecting increasing emphasis on enforcing schemas, guardrails, and structured tool invocation in autonomous Agent loops. Most notably, _Workflow, UI & API Behavior_ (GT6) becomes the single most prevalent topic through 2025, driven largely by Visual Workflow Platforms such as Dify, LangFlow, and Flowise. This surge indicates a transition toward UI-driven and low-code Agent development, where failures increasingly arise from workflow composition, API semantics, and user interaction rather than core model execution. Taken together, Stack Overflow captures early learning and configuration challenges, while GitHub reflects later-stage operational and ecosystem-level friction. Rather than diminishing over time, Agent difficulty shifts upward in the stack: as execution becomes more stable, challenges concentrate around orchestration, policy enforcement, and user-facing workflows, including application-level Agents used to compose and manage end-to-end workflows. This progression underscores that AI Agent complexity is rooted not only in models, but in autonomy, persistent state, and cross-system coordination at scale.

#### Technologies in Practice.

Tables [7](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#S3.T7 "Table 7 ‣ Domain-Level Difficulty and Resolution Dynamics. ‣ 3.3 RQ3: Domains of the AI Agent Ecosystem ‣ 3 Results ‣ What Challenges Do Developers Face in AI Agent Systems? An Empirical Study on Stack Overflow & GitHub Issues") and [8](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#S3.T8 "Table 8 ‣ Domain-Level Difficulty and Resolution Dynamics. ‣ 3.3 RQ3: Domains of the AI Agent Ecosystem ‣ 3 Results ‣ What Challenges Do Developers Face in AI Agent Systems? An Empirical Study on Stack Overflow & GitHub Issues") summarize the AI Agent technologies observed in our Stack Overflow and GitHub datasets. On GitHub, Langflow, Dify, and LangChain appear as the most prominent repositories in our sample, each exceeding 120K stars and associated with large volumes of issues related to orchestration, workflows, and integrations. Stack Overflow discussion is more concentrated, with LangChain and LangGraph accounting for the majority of Agent-related posts, followed by LlamaIndex and AutoGen (Table [7](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#S3.T7 "Table 7 ‣ Domain-Level Difficulty and Resolution Dynamics. ‣ 3.3 RQ3: Domains of the AI Agent Ecosystem ‣ 3 Results ‣ What Challenges Do Developers Face in AI Agent Systems? An Empirical Study on Stack Overflow & GitHub Issues")). While we examined additional Agent technologies identified on GitHub, many lacked stable Stack Overflow tags or sufficient post volume to support reliable, low-noise analysis. This reflects a Python-centric focus in Stack Overflow discussions, contrasted with greater language and platform diversity on GitHub (Table [9](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#S3.T9 "Table 9 ‣ Domain-Level Difficulty and Resolution Dynamics. ‣ 3.3 RQ3: Domains of the AI Agent Ecosystem ‣ 3 Results ‣ What Challenges Do Developers Face in AI Agent Systems? An Empirical Study on Stack Overflow & GitHub Issues")).

https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2/topics_overtime_paper_ready.pngFigure 5: GitHub Issues topics overtime.

AI Agent Discussions Over Time.

Comparing to Other Fields.
As shown in Table [11](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#S4.T11 "Table 11 ‣ Technologies in Practice. ‣ 4 Discussion ‣ What Challenges Do Developers Face in AI Agent Systems? An Empirical Study on Stack Overflow & GitHub Issues") and prior studies Ahmed and Bagherzadeh ( [2018](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib3 "")); Rosen and Shihab ( [2016](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib39 "")); Yang et al. ( [2016](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib51 "")); Bagherzadeh and Khatchadourian ( [2019](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib9 "")); Abdellatif et al. ( [2020](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib1 "")), AI Agent topics are characterized by _high difficulty_: they have the highest share of unanswered questions and the longest time-to-accept. While their popularity is comparable to established domains, fewer answers per question indicate slower community convergence. Overall, AI Agents combine strong engagement with above-average resolution difficulty.

## 5 Related Work

Stack Overflow vs. ChatGPT.
Helcic and Santos helic2025stack reported that the introduction of ChatGPT significantly influenced Stack Overflow activity—leading to longer and more complex questions and answers, as well as increased code length and question difficulty. They conclude that ChatGPT has effectively raised the overall bar for content complexity and challenge on the platform. Kabir et al. kabir2024stack found that participants in their study preferred human-generated Stack Overflow answers in 65.18% of cases. Similarly, Liu et al. liu2023better found that, in debugging tasks, ChatGPT performed worse than Stack Overflow, and users interacting with ChatGPT took longer between their first execution.

Stack Overflow Data & LDA Topic Modeling.
Previous work has mined Stack Overflow at multiple levels: from user commenting behavior and community dynamics zhang2019reading, to downstream effects such as the impact of Stack Overflow–sourced code on mobile apps abdalkareem2017code and automated generation of code comments from snippets aghajani2019automated. In parallel, researchers have used topic modeling on unstructured software repository data to systematically surface developer pain points bridge2011unstructured, and applied LDA to Stack Overflow itself to map what developers discuss at scale Barua et al. ( [2014](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib10 "")).
Continuing this mindset, domain-specific studies have applied similar methods to particular ecosystems: Rosen et al. Rosen and Shihab ( [2016](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib39 "")) used topic modeling on Stack Overflow to categorize mobile-development challenges (six topics); Uddin et al. Uddin et al. ( [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib47 "")) analyzed IoT questions with an empirical, topic-driven approach; Abdellatif et al. Abdellatif et al. ( [2020](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib1 "")) studied chatbot development, organizing five categories across twelve topics; Openja et al. openja2020analysis examined modern release engineering practices; Elshan et al. Elshan et al. ( [2024](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib19 "")) unveiled challenges in low-code software development (ten topics); Haque et al. Haque et al. ( [2020](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib23 "")) documented Docker-related issues; and Wang et al. wang2023automl studied AutoML from a software-engineering perspective. AI and machine learning for software engineering also have been explored within development practices and data-driven insights at scale alshangiti2019developing; Sayyadnejad et al. Sayyadnejad et al. ( [2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib41 "")) also identified ten topics about challenges of building explainable AI systems. Complementary studies have manually classified themes in Q&A platforms—e.g., MATLAB user issues naghashzadeh2021users, technical debt discussions alfayez2023asked, and code smells/anti-patterns in practice Tahir et al. ( [2018](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib45 "")). Building on two established pathways—automated topic modeling (e.g., LDA) and manual qualitative analysis—prior work has demonstrated that both can provide rich taxonomies of developer challenges. For example, Chen et al. ( [2020](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib14 "")) manually derived 25 topics and 72 subtopics related to the implementation of deep-learning-based software. In the same spirit, our study deliberately combines a community-standard validated approach for Stack Overflow data (LDA-Mallet McCallum ( [2002](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib31 ""))) Abdellatif et al. ( [2020](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib1 "")); Li et al. ( [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib28 "")); Sayyadnejad et al. ( [2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib41 "")), with a targeted manual analysis to refine subtopics and capture nuance—a strategy aligned with standard mixed-method empirical studies such as Sayyadnejad et al. ( [2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib41 "")); Uddin et al. ( [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib47 "")); Mahmood et al. ( [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib30 "")).

AI Agents. Cemri et al. ( [2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib13 "")) derive a data-driven taxonomy of multi-Agent LLM failures— _specification_, _inter-Agent misalignment_, _task verification_—aligned to pre /execution/post stages via manual coding of 200+200{+} end-to-end traces. Different studies focus on distinct AI Agent challenges, including Security Deng et al. ( [2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib16 "")); Zou et al. ( [2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib52 "")); de Witt ( [2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib48 "")), Ethics & governance Gabriel et al. ( [2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib21 "")); Kolt ( [2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib26 "")), Foundations & effectiveness of multi-Agent AI systems (MAS) Tian et al. ( [2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib46 "")); Du and Ding ( [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib18 "")); Du et al. ( [2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib17 "")); Sapkota et al. ( [2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib40 "")), and Design & applications Mo et al. ( [2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib33 "")); Shetty et al. ( [2024](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib44 "")); Cemri et al. ( [2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib13 "")). And also Schneider ( [2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib42 "")) in a conceptual survey clarify how Agentic AI extends GenAI (reasoning, tools, memory, interaction), formalize agent specification, and catalog open challenges.

Security.Deng et al. ( [2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib16 "")) survey the agent threat surface (intra-execution, agent–env, agent–agent, memory) and defenses (debate, RAG, constraints, post-correction, tool-use auditing). Zou et al. ( [2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib52 "")) show near-universal, transferable prompt-injection/policy-violation attacks in large-scale red teaming, exposing defense gaps. de Witt ( [2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib48 "")) argue for _multi-agent security_ as a distinct field, outlining open problems (collusion, attribution, cascade dynamics) and a research agenda. Ethics & governance.Gabriel et al. ( [2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib21 "")) call for new ethics for autonomous agents—safety, alignment, and social-coordination risks as autonomy grows. Kolt ( [2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib26 "")) frame governance as a principal–agent problem and advocate transparency, incentive design, monitoring, and accountability across developers/deployers/users. Foundations & effectiveness of MAS.Tian et al. ( [2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib46 "")) formalize multi-agent AI, showing gains from task reallocation and diverse agents, but error amplification under overlap/misalignment; emphasize feedback that reshapes topology. Du and Ding ( [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib18 "")) survey multi-agent deep reinforcement learning, stressing non-stationarity, partial observability, scalability, and credit assignment. Du et al. ( [2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib17 "")) review agent–agent communication, distilling five pillars (scalability, security, real-time, performance, manageability) and standardization paths. Design & applications.Mo et al. ( [2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib33 "")) study an interactive refactoring agent, reporting decision strategies and collaboration principles. Shetty et al. ( [2024](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib44 "")) sketch requirements and a prototype (AIOpsLab) for a standardized framework to build/test/compare cloud-ops agents.

Novelty

We are the first study to map the challenges of AI Agent systems from practitioners’ discussions on Stack Overflow. Using validated LDA–MALLET, we recover seven challenge topics and, via human subtopic coding, 77 finer issues. We introduce standard, comparable popularity and difficulty metrics and rank challenges—something prior surveys/conceptual works could not quantify—then provide the first longitudinal view (2021→2025) with event-aligned interpretation, linking the mid-2023 take-off to model-API releases (Fig. [5](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#S4.F5 "Figure 5 ‣ Technologies in Practice. ‣ 4 Discussion ‣ What Challenges Do Developers Face in AI Agent Systems? An Empirical Study on Stack Overflow & GitHub Issues")). We also quantify technology share at scale (Table LABEL:tab:AIAgentTechnologies). Finally, a cross-domain comparison with five prior SO areas (mobile, security, big data, IoT, chatbot) (Table [11](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#S4.T11 "Table 11 ‣ Technologies in Practice. ‣ 4 Discussion ‣ What Challenges Do Developers Face in AI Agent Systems? An Empirical Study on Stack Overflow & GitHub Issues")). Collectively, this yields a practitioner-grounded, metric-driven map of where AI Agent work is hard—and how those pressures shift with tooling milestones.

## 6 Implications and Recommendations

Our findings suggest that the primary difficulties in AI Agent development do not stem from model capability alone, but from the interaction between autonomous control flow, persistent state, tool invocation, and user-facing workflows. Based on the observed challenge distributions across Stack Overflow, GitHub, ecosystem domains, and time, we derive the following implications for Agent designers, framework builders, and researchers.

(1) Treat orchestration as a first-class architectural concern.
Across both Stack Overflow (ST4) and GitHub (GT12), orchestration-related challenges are consistently among the most difficult, despite relatively lower visibility on Q&A platforms. This aligns strongly with _Planning_ and _Multi-Agent_ design patterns, where Agents explicitly decompose goals into sub-tasks, route control between steps or sub-Agents, and coordinate execution over time rather than issuing a single model call. Failures in these patterns typically arise from task decomposition, routing, and execution order rather than individual model outputs. Frameworks should therefore expose orchestration primitives explicitly—state transitions, retries, and termination conditions—rather than embedding them implicitly inside chains or graphs. Clear execution semantics and debuggable control flow are essential for scaling beyond single-step Agents.

(2) Stabilize interaction contracts between models, tools, and memory.
A large fraction of unresolved issues concentrate around structured inputs and outputs (ST7, GT3, GT10), reflecting fragility in prompt schemas, tool-calling formats, and validation rules. These challenges directly affect _ReAct_ and _Tool Use_ patterns, in which an Agent alternates between reasoning steps and external actions (e.g., API calls, database queries), and where even minor contract violations can halt entire execution loops. The rise of Model Context Protocol (MCP)–style abstractions is a promising direction: explicit, versioned contracts for context, tools, and memory can reduce silent failures and make Agent behavior more predictable across providers and environments.

(3) Design memory and retrieval as evolving systems, not static components.
Retrieval- and memory-related topics (ST2, ST6, GT2, GT13) are consistently among the hardest to resolve and show long-lived issue activity. This reflects the mismatch between static RAG pipelines and the needs of long-running, stateful Agents. Patterns such as _Reflection_ and _Planning_ assume that Agents can reliably store intermediate results, revisit prior decisions, and update internal state over time—assumptions that current tooling often fails to guarantee. We therefore recommend treating Agent memory as a lifecycle-managed subsystem, with explicit policies for updating, pruning, and validating state, rather than as a passive vector store queried once per task.

(4) Expect UI- and workflow-centric Agents to dominate future development effort.
Over time, GitHub activity increasingly concentrates in _Workflow, UI & API Behavior_ (GT6), particularly within visual workflow platforms such as Dify, Langflow, and Flowise. These systems operationalize Agentic design patterns by allowing users to compose planning, tool use, and multi-step execution through graphical or declarative workflows, often to build other Agents or application-level pipelines. While many UI-related issues resolve quickly, their sheer volume indicates that the next phase of Agent adoption is driven by end-user composition rather than low-level model access. This suggests that Agent frameworks must prioritize explainability, safe defaults, and recoverability at the workflow level.

(5) Application-level Agents amplify upstream design decisions.
Agent applications aggregate failures from across the stack, including runtime execution (GT11), platform integrations (GT1), provider inconsistencies (GT13), and unhandled exceptions (GT8). In design-pattern terms, application-level Agents frequently combine _Tool Use_, _Planning_, and _Multi-Agent_ behaviors into a single system, making them especially sensitive to upstream instability. Our results indicate that robustness at the application layer depends less on adding local safeguards and more on improving guarantees in orchestration, interaction contracts, and memory management below.

Implications for Practitioners.
For practitioners building and deploying AI Agents, our findings emphasize that most failures originate at system boundaries rather than within individual model calls. Issues related to orchestration, runtime execution, and workflow behavior dominate GitHub activity, especially in application-level Agents and visual workflow platforms. Practitioners should therefore prioritize explicit control flow, strong observability, and defensive execution strategies over increasing Agent autonomy. In practice, this means treating planning loops, retries, and tool invocation as auditable system components, and investing early in workflow-level safeguards when deploying user-facing or application-based Agents.

Implications for Educators.
For educators, the results reveal a gap between what learners most frequently encounter and what they find hardest to master. While installation, prompting, and basic RAG pipelines attract the most attention, the most difficult challenges involve orchestration, persistent memory, and long-running execution. Educational material should therefore move beyond linear prompt–response examples and instead teach Agent behavior over time, including failure modes, execution traces, and debugging practices across Agentic design patterns such as ReAct, Planning, and Multi-Agent coordination.

Implications for Researchers.
For researchers, the findings suggest that progress in AI Agents depends less on improving standalone model capabilities and more on formalizing system-level abstractions. Persistent difficulty in orchestration, retrieval, and interaction contracts indicates a need for research on execution semantics, memory lifecycle management, and standardized interfaces between models and tools. Emerging efforts such as Model Context Protocols point toward promising directions, but our data shows that these abstractions must be evaluated under long-running, user-facing conditions. Future research should therefore prioritize reproducible Agent failures, coordination mechanisms, and cross-component guarantees rather than isolated reasoning benchmarks.

Overall Implication.
Taken together, these findings indicate that AI Agent development is transitioning from a model-centric phase to a systems-engineering phase. The dominant challenges now arise from autonomy, persistent state, and cross-component coordination rather than prompt quality alone. Agentic design patterns remain useful abstractions, but their practical success increasingly depends on explicit orchestration semantics, stable interaction protocols, and tooling that supports long-running, user-facing workflows.

## 7 Threats To Validity

Selection of data source.
Following previous work Haque et al. ( [2020](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib23 "")); alshangiti2019developing; Ahmed and Bagherzadeh ( [2018](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib3 "")); Yang et al. ( [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib50 "")); Elshan et al. ( [2024](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib19 "")); Ahmed et al. ( [2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib2 "")); Bi et al. ( [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib11 "")); Rosen and Shihab ( [2016](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib39 "")), we use Stack Overflow as our sole data source. This choice is a potential threat, since Stack Overflow may not capture all developer questions and we may miss insights from other venues. However, Stack Overflow is a widely respected community with many participating developers, including experts, which we believe minimizes this risk. In addition, AI Agent is a relatively new area, and it is not easy to find substantive discussions on other platforms.

Selection of tags. Relying on tags to collect Stack Overflow questions and answers is a validity threat, as tagging can be incomplete, and some relevant posts may be missed. To reduce this risk, we followed well-established techniques Ahmed and Bagherzadeh ( [2018](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib3 "")); Rosen and Shihab ( [2016](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib39 "")); Yang et al. ( [2016](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib51 "")); Haque et al. ( [2020](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib23 "")). However, the creation of our tag set could introduce bias, as the selected tags may not cover all AI Agent–related questions, and the final tag set could be influenced by individual experiences. To mitigate this, we engaged the first two researchers in the validation process to enhance the reliability and validity of the final tag selection.

Construction of topic taxonomy.

We assumed that LDA’s probabilistic topic modeling adequately captures the latent themes in our corpus. While LDA is widely used in software engineering, it has known stochastic variation; to mitigate this, we ran multiple seeds/iterations and selected configurations by coherence to ensure stability Ahmed et al. ( [2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib2 "")). Recently, LLM-based approaches such as BERTopic have gained traction azher2024limtopic; bu2023software; naghshzan2023enhancing; Ahmed et al. ( [2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib2 "")), leveraging transformer embeddings for richer semantics. However, prior work notes transfer gaps on technical SE text with code/jargon azher2024limtopic; zhang2023unifying; Ahmed et al. ( [2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib2 "")) and well-documented challenges on _short_ and _small_ corpora—exactly our setting (final dataset: 3,191 posts)—including sparse context, clustering/UMAP instability, and variability across domains/languages fan2023topic; de2022experiments; medvecki2023multilingual. Practitioner reports also highlight excessive “outliers” and run-to-run drift in the UMAP–HDBSCAN pipeline grootendorst2022issue461, and recent analyses link outliers to topic drift in evolving corpora zve2025outliers. While refinements (e.g., LLM-guided topic repair) are emerging chang2024large, there is not yet a standard, reproducible recipe for SE datasets of this size. By contrast, LDA has a long record of successful, documented use in SE bajaj2014mining; Barua et al. ( [2014](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib10 "")); Yang et al. ( [2016](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib51 "")); Rosen and Shihab ( [2016](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib39 "")); Sayyadnejad et al. ( [2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib41 "")). Given these trade-offs, we adopt LDA for interpretability, comparability with prior work, and reproducibility, leaving BERTopic-style modeling as future work.

Manual labeling. We manually reviewed a stratified, confidence-aware sample (unlike purely random sampling in much prior work), weighting by each topic’s size and the model’s per-record confidence, and assigned topics labels following established protocols Sayyadnejad et al. ( [2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib41 "")); Uddin et al. ( [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib47 "")); Mahmood et al. ( [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib30 "")); Chen et al. ( [2020](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25423v2#bib.bib14 "")); while some bias is possible, two authors performed analyzing and reconciliation, and a third author independently validated the final labels.

## 8 Conclusion

This study presents a large-scale study on technical challenges developers face when constructing AI Agents, based on large-scale data from Stack Overflow and GitHub. The findings indicate that Agent development is primarily a systems-engineering challenge rather than solely a limitation of model capability. Analysis of Stack Overflow data identifies seven recurring Agent challenge topics, further divided into 28 fine-grained subtopics, encompassing installation, retrieval, orchestration, interaction contracts, and runtime reliability. A complementary examination of GitHub issues reveals 13 Agent-related challenges, illustrating how these problems arise during framework development, integration, and maintenance. Across both platforms, configuration and prompting issues receive the most attention and are typically resolved rapidly, while challenges related to retrieval pipelines, orchestration semantics, and long-running execution remain the most difficult to address. We noticed the most challenging Agent issues are not the most frequently discussed and tend to have higher rates of unresolved questions and longer resolution times. Domain-level analysis further revealed infrastructure-oriented domains accumulate fewer but longer-running issues, whereas UI- and workflow-centric platforms generate a higher volume of faster-resolving reports. Application-level Agents amplify failures throughout the stack, demonstrating that upstream design decisions in orchestration, memory, and interaction contracts propagate to user-facing systems. Progress in AI Agents relies less on improving individual models and more on enhancing orchestration mechanisms, stabilizing interaction contracts, and managing persistent state across complex, tool-mediated workflows.

## YouTube Video Transcripts (from Article Guidelines)

<details>
<summary>Real Agents vs. Workflows: The Truth Behind AI 'Agents'</summary>

# Real Agents vs. Workflows: The Truth Behind AI 'Agents'

Okay, I will provide a detailed and enriched transcript of the video as requested.

[ 00:00 ] (Video begins with a cartoon of a businessman talking to a robot, the robot has "IMPOSTER" written above its head) What most people call agents aren't agents. I've never really liked the term agent, until I saw this recent article by Anthropic, (The video cuts to a screenshot of an Anthropic article titled "Building effective agents") where I totally agree and now see how we can call something an agent. (The video cuts to a black screen with text "MOST AGENTS ARE JUST API CALL TO LLM" and a robot with an API call box) The vast majority is simply API calls to a language model. This is this, (The API call box expands to show a Python code snippet using OpenAI API) a few lines of code and a prompt. (Text "Can't act independently" appears at the bottom left) This cannot act independently, (Text "Can't make decisions" appears at the bottom right) make decisions, or do anything. (Text "Just replies to users" appears at the bottom center) It simply replies to your users. Still, we call them agents. (The robot is replaced by a man sitting at a desk with two monitors) But this isn't what we need. (Text "WE NEED REAL AGENTS" appears) We need real agents. (Text "But what is a real agent?" appears) But what is a real agent?

[ 00:30 ] Before we dive into serious agentic stuff, if you are a student, writer, blogger or content creator like me, (The video splits into three panels showing a student reading, a man working on a laptop, and a man talking in an office) or would like to help becoming one, you will love the sponsor of today's video, (The screen transitions to a purple background with "Originality.ai" logo) with a clever name, Originality.ai. (The video shows the Originality.ai website homepage) Originality.ai is an awesome tool designed to detect AI-generated content, (The video highlights "Accurate AI Detection") check for plagiarism, (The video highlights "Plagiarism Checking") grammar, readability, and even fact-check your work. (The video highlights "Fact Checking Aid") Everything you need to publish with integrity. (The video shows the Originality.ai content scanner interface) Simply upload a document, (A document is uploaded to the scanner) in seconds, it flags any AI-generated text, (AI-generated text is highlighted in red) highlights plagiarism, (Plagiarism check shows "No matches") and even checks grammar and readability with many useful tips and suggestions. (Grammar check shows "98.04% Correct" and readability score is displayed) I really love this feature. [ 01:00 ] (The video shows the fact-checking feature) It also offers fact-checking, ensuring every claim in your content stands up to scrutiny. Pretty cool when you work on important or technical work. (The video returns to the man at the desk) All based on the most state-of-the-art language models and systems. Try Originality.ai today with the first link in the description. (Text "LINK IS IN THE DESCRIPTION" appears at the bottom)

[ 01:29 ] So let's start over. (A query box and an LLM box appear on a black background) We have an LLM accessed programmatically, which is through an API or access locally in your own server or machine. And then what? Well, we needed it to take action or do something more than just generate text. How? By giving it access to tools and their documentation. (A SQL Query box appears, with an arrow from Query to it) We give them access to a tool like the ability to execute SQL queries in a database to access private knowledge. Specifically, we code all that ourselves to have our LLM generate SQL queries. (Blocks "Infer Schema" and "Construct SQL Query" appear within SQL Query box) And then, our code will send and execute the query automatically in our database. (Blocks "Execute SQL Query", "Self Correct", and "Optimize" appear, with an arrow to "BigQuery Tables") We then send back the outputs, so that it uses them to answer the user. (An "Answer" box appears, with arrows from LLM and SQL Query) This is what another great proportion of people call agents. [ 02:00 ] (Text "NOT AN AGENT" appears repeatedly in the background) They are still not agents. This is simply a process hardcoded, or with small variations like routers that we discuss in the course. (Text "WORKFLOW" appears in the background) Of course, it's useful and it's super powerful. Yet, it's not an intelligent being or something independent. It's not an agent acting on our behalf. It's simply a program we made and control, or as Anthropic calls it, a workflow. Don't get me wrong. A workflow is pretty damn useful, and it can be quite complex and advanced. We can implement intelligent routers to decide what tool to use and when to give it access to various databases. (Blocks "LLM Call Router", "LLM Call 1", "LLM Call 2", and "LLM Call 3" appear in a flowchart) Have it decide which one to query and when. Have it execute tasks through action tools, through code, and more. Plus, you can have as many workflows as you wish. [ 03:00 ] Yet, I simply want to state how different it is than an actual agent, the type of agent we dream of and the type Ilya mentioned at a recent talk I attended at NeurIPS. (The video shows a YouTube video of Ilya Sutskever talking) So right now we have our incredible language models and the unbelievable chatbot and they can even do things but they're also kind of strangely unreliable and they get confused when while also having dramatically superhuman performance on eval so it's really unclear how to reconcile this. But eventually sooner or later the following will be achieved: those systems are actually going to be agentic in a real ways whereas right now the systems are not agents in any meaningful sense just very that might be too strong they're very very slightly agentic. Just the beginning. (The video returns to the man at the desk, with text "WHAT EXACTLY IS A 'REAL AGENT'?" appearing) The next natural question might be: what exactly is a real agent? In simple terms, a real agent is something that functions independently. (Text "- Something that functions independently" appears) [ 03:50 ] More specifically, it's something capable of employing processes like our system 2 thinking. (An image of a human brain with "SYSTEM 2" on the left appears, along with text "- Reason", "- Reflect", "- Recognize lack of knowledge") Able to genuinely reason, reflect, and recognize when it lacks knowledge. This is almost the opposite of our system 1 thinking, (An image of a human brain with "SYSTEM 1" on the right appears, along with text "- Fast", "- Automatic", "- Based purely on patterns and learned response") which is fast, automatic, and based purely on patterns and learned responses. Like reflexes when you need to catch a dropping glass. (Text "i.e. Reflexes while catching the dropping glass" appears) By contrast, system 2 thinking might involve deciding whether to prevent the glass from falling in the first place, perhaps by using a nearby tool like a tray or moving the fragile object out of the way. (Text "i.e. Thinking to prevent the glass from falling" appears below "Reason") A real agent then will not only know how to use tools, but also decide when and why to use them based on deliberate reasoning. (Text "A real agent deliberately decides when and why to use tools with deliberate reasoning" appears below the brains)

[ 04:40 ] (The video shows screenshots of OpenAI o1 and o3-mini announcements) OpenAI's new o1 and o3 series exemplified this shift, as they begin exploring system 2-like approaches (The video shows a group of people discussing with text "System 2-like approach") and try to make models reason by first discussing with themselves internally, (A text bubble with a query and "Thinking" and "Reason internally before answering" appears) mimicking a human-like approach to reasoning before speaking. (The thought process is elaborated, showing "Understanding the scenario", "Evaluating outcomes", "Tracking the strawberry's path" before providing a detailed answer) Unlike traditional language models that rely on next-word or next-token prediction, (A transformer architecture diagram is shown, highlighting "Next-word prediction") essentially a system 1 instant thinking mechanism, purely based on what it knows and learned to guess the next instant thing to go with no plan. (The diagram shows how the model predicts the next words in a sentence) These new models aim to incorporate deeper reasoning capabilities, making a move toward the deliberate, reflective thinking associated with system 2. (The video returns to the man at the desk) Something required for a true agent to be. But we are diverging a bit too much with this Kahneman parenthesis. Let me clarify what I mean by a real agent by going back to workflows and what they really are.

[ 05:30 ] (The video transitions to a blue background with "WORKFLOW" text) Workflows follow specific code lines and integrations. (Text "- Follow specific code lines and integrations" appears) And other than the LLMs outputs, are pretty predictable. (Text "- Predictable output" appears) They are responsible for most of the advanced applications you see and use today, and for a reason. (Text "- Responsible for most of the advanced applications" appears) They are consistent, more predictable, and incredibly powerful when leveraged properly. (Texts "- Consistent", "- More predictable", "- Incredibly powerful" appear) As Anthropic wrote, (The video shows a screenshot of the Anthropic article again) workflows are systems where LLMs and tools are orchestrated through predefined code paths. (The sentence "Workflows are systems where LLMs and tools are orchestrated through predefined code paths" is highlighted) Here's what a workflow looks like. (The video returns to the black background with the flowchart of the SQL Query example) We have our LLM, some tools or memory to retrieve for additional context, iterate a bit with multiple calls to the LLM, and then an output sent back to the user. As we discussed, when a system needs to sometimes do a task and sometimes another, depending on conditions, workflows can use a router. (The "LLM Call Router" block is highlighted) With various conditions to select the right tool or the right prompt to use. They can even work in parallel to be more efficient. Better, we can have some sort of main model, which we refer to as an orchestrator, that selects all the different fellow models to call for specific tasks and synthesizes the results, such as our SQL example, where we'd have the main orchestrator getting the user query and could decide if it needs to query a data set or not, and if it does, ask the SQL agent to generate the SQL query and query the data set and get it back and synthesize the final answer, thanks to all the information provided. (The flowchart shows the entire process) This is a workflow.

[ 06:30 ] (Text "WORKFLOW" appears in the background) Just like ChatGPT is a workflow, (The video shows a screenshot of OpenAI's "Introducing Canvas" article) sometimes using Canvas and sometimes just straight up answering your question. Even if complex and advanced, it is still all hardcoded. (The video returns to the man at the desk) If you know what you need your system to do, (Text "If you know what you need your system to do" appears) you need a workflow. (Text "YOU NEED A WORKFLOW" appears) However advanced it may be. (The video shows a screenshot of CreAI's "Sample Use Cases" with various agents like "Sales", "Marketing", "Analytics", "Finance", "Technology") For instance, what CreAI calls agents function like predefined workflows assigned to specific tasks. (The text below "Marketing", "Analytics", "Finance", and "Technology" is highlighted, showcasing how they are workflows) (The video shows a screenshot of Anthropic's "Building effective agents" article again) While Anthropic envisions an agent as a single system capable of reasoning through any task independently. (The sentence "Single system capable of reasoning through any task independently" is highlighted in green) Both approaches have merit. (The video shows a comparison between "WORKFLOW" and "AGENT" categories) One is predictable and intuitive, (Text "- Predictable", "- Intuitive" appear under "WORKFLOW") while the other aims for flexibility and adaptability. (Text "- Flexibility", "- Adaptability" appear under "AGENT") However, the latter is far harder to achieve with current models, (Text "- Far harder to achieve" appears under "AGENT") and better fits an agent definition to me. (Text "- Easier to achieve" appears under "WORKFLOW") So about those real agents. (The sentence "Agents, on the other hand, are systems where LLMs dynamically direct their own processes and tool usage, maintaining control over how they accomplish tasks." from the Anthropic article is highlighted again) Agents, on the other hand, are systems where LLMs dynamically direct their own processes and tool usage, maintaining control over how they accomplish tasks themselves. This is what Anthropic wrote. And it is what I agree the most with. (The video returns to the man at the desk, with "REAL AGENTS" text appearing) Real agents make a plan by exchanging with you and understanding your needs. (Text "- Make a plan by exchanging with user", "- Understand user needs" appear) [ 08:00 ] It iterating at a reasoning level to decide on the steps to take to solve the problem or query. (Text "- Iterate at a 'reasoning' level to decide on steps for solution" appears) Ideally, it will even ask you if it needs more information or clarification instead of hallucinating, as with current LLMs. (Text "- Ask user if it needs more information or clarification instead of hallucinating" appears) Still, they cannot be simply built. They require a very powerful LLM, better than those we have now, and an environment to evolve in, like a discussion with you and some extra powers like tools that they can use themselves whenever they see fit and iterate. (A flowchart shows "Human" interacting with "LLM Call", which takes "Action" in an "Environment" and receives "Feedback", with a "Stop" option) In short, you can see agents almost as replacing someone or a role (Text "AGENT - Replace someone or a role" appears) and a workflow replacing a task one would do. (Text "WORKFLOW - Replace a task one would do" appears) There is no hardcoded path. The agentic system will make its decisions. They are much more advanced and complex things that we still haven't built very successfully yet. [ 09:00 ] This independence and trust in your system obviously makes it more susceptible to failures, (Text "More susceptible to failures" appears) more expensive to run and use, (Text "More expensive to run & use" appears) add latency, (Text "Added latency" appears) and worst of all, the results aren't that exciting now. (Text "Boring results" appears) When they are, they are completely inconsistent. So what is an actual good example of an agent? Two examples that quickly come to my mind are Devin's and Anthropic's computer use. (Text "ANTHROPIC" and "Devin" appear in separate boxes) Yet, they are for now disappointing agents. (The "Devin" box remains on screen) If you're curious about Devin, there's a really good blog from Hamel Husain sharing his experience using it. (The video shows a screenshot of Hamel Husain's blog) Devin offers an intriguing glimpse into the promise and challenges of agent-based systems. (The video shows Devin's workspace with a query) From now on, Devin is in the driver's seat. (Devin creates a plan) First, Devin makes a step-by-step plan of how to tackle the problem (Devin uses a browser and a command line interface to work on code) using all the same tools that a human software engineer would use. (Devin continues to work on code) Devin has its own command line, its own computing environment and independently handles tasks like API integrations and real-time problem-solving. [ 10:00 ] In this case, Devin decides to use the browser to pull up API documentation so that it can read up and learn how to plug into each of these APIs. (Devin continues working, opening multiple browser tabs for documentation) However, as Hamel's extensive testing demonstrated, while Devin excelled at simpler, well-defined tasks, things that we can usually do quite easily, it struggled with complex or autonomous ones, often providing overcomplicated solutions and pursuing unfeasible paths, whereas advanced workflows like Cursor (The video shows a screenshot of "The AI Code Editor" by Cursor) don't have as many issues. These limitations reflect the broader challenges of building reliable, context-aware agents with current LLMs, even if you raise millions and millions. Here, Devin aligns more with Anthropic's vision, (Text "Devin" appears in a box) showcasing the promise and challenges of a reasoning agent. It can autonomously tackle complex problems but struggle with inconsistency. By contrast, workflows like those inspired by CrewAI (Text "crewAI" appears in a box) are simpler and more robust for specific tasks, but lack the flexibility of true reasoning systems. Similarly, we have Anthropic's ambitious attempt at creating an autonomous agent having access to our computer. (Text "ANTHROPIC" appears in a box) Anthropic computer use. (The video shows a simulation of Anthropic's computer use with a virtual desktop) Which had lots of hype when it first came out and has since been quite forgotten. [ 10:59 ] The system was undeniably complex and embodied the characteristics of a true agent. Autonomous decision-making, dynamic tool usage, and the ability to interact with its environment. Its goal was also to replace anyone on a computer. Quite promising, or scary. Still, its decline also serves as a reminder of the challenges in creating practical agentic systems that not only work as intended, but do so systematically. In short, LLMs are simply not ready yet for becoming true agents, but it may be the case soon. For now, as with all things code-related, we should always aim to find a solution to our problem that is as simple as possible, (Text "Find a solution that is as simple as possible" appears) one that we can iterate easily and debug easily. Simple LLM calls are often the way to go. And it is often what people and companies sell as being an agent. But you won't be fooled anymore. You may want to complement LLMs with some external knowledge (Text "Complement LLMs with RAG or fine-tuning" appears) through the use of retrieval systems or light fine-tuning, but your money and time aiming for true agents (Text "Save your money and time for complex problems that we can't solve" appears) should be saved for really complex problems that cannot be solved otherwise.

[ 11:51 ] I hope this video helped you understand the difference between workflows and a real agent, and when to use both. If you found it useful, please share it with a friend in the AI community, and don't forget to subscribe for more in-depth AI content. Thank you for watching. (The video ends with social media handles and the channel logo)

***
_Summary: This video clarifies the distinction between "agents" and "workflows" in the context of Large Language Models (LLMs), arguing that most systems currently labeled as "agents" are, in fact, workflows. It defines a "real agent" as a system capable of independent reasoning and decision-making, similar to human System 2 thinking, and highlights the current limitations of LLMs in achieving true agentic behavior while acknowledging the utility of well-designed workflows._

</details>

</golden_source>

<golden_source type="guideline_urls">
## Additional Sources Scraped (from Article Guidelines)

<details>
<summary>A Developer’s Guide to Building Scalable AI: Workflows vs Agents</summary>

# A Developer’s Guide to Building Scalable AI: Workflows vs Agents

**Source URL:** <https://towardsdatascience.com/a-developers-guide-to-building-scalable-ai-workflows-vs-agents/>

Understanding the architectural trade-offs between autonomous agents and orchestrated workflows — because someone needs to make this decision, and it might as well be you

[Hailey Quach](https://towardsdatascience.com/author/hailey-quach/)

Jun 27, 2025

38 min read

https://towardsdatascience.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/06/agent-vs-workflow.jpegImage by author

There was a time not long ago — okay, like three months ago — when I fell deep into the agent rabbit hole.

I had just started experimenting with CrewAI and LangGraph, and it felt like I’d unlocked a whole new dimension of building. Suddenly, I didn’t just have tools and pipelines — I had _crews_. I could spin up agents that could reason, plan, talk to tools, and talk to each other. Multi-agent systems! Agents that summon other agents! I was practically architecting the AI version of a startup team.

Every use case became a candidate for a crew. Meeting prep? Crew. Slide generation? Crew. Lab report review? Crew.

It was exciting — until it wasn’t.

The more I built, the more I ran into questions I hadn’t thought through: _How do I monitor this? How do I debug a loop where the agent just keeps “thinking”? What happens when something breaks? Can anyone else even maintain this with me?_

That’s when I realized I had skipped a crucial question: _Did this really need to be agentic?_ Or was I just excited to use the shiny new thing?

Since then, I’ve become a lot more cautious — and a lot more practical. Because there’s a big difference (according to [Anthropic](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/building-effective-agents)) between:

- A **workflow**: a structured LLM pipeline with clear control flow, where you define the steps — use a tool, retrieve context, call the model, handle the output.
- And an **agent**: an autonomous system where the LLM decides what to do next, which tools to use, and when it’s “done.”

Workflows are more like you calling the shots and the LLM following your lead. Agents are more like hiring a brilliant, slightly chaotic intern who figures things out on their own — sometimes beautifully, sometimes in terrifyingly expensive ways.

This article is for anyone who’s ever felt that same temptation to build a multi-agent empire before thinking through what it takes to maintain it. It’s not a warning, it’s a reality check — and a field guide. Because there _are_ times when agents are exactly what you need. But most of the time? You just need a solid workflow.

* * *
